topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

The shared file, a grainy photo from a forgotten summer scrapbook, landed on my desk with a soft thud. Beside it sat Sarah’s name, underlined twice. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. I stared at the picture—a group of grinning kids in brightly colored, ill-fitting uniforms, surrounded by towering, suspiciously cheerful cabins. "No way," I muttered, my voice tight. "We were at Camp Whispering Pines?" The memory surfaced, unwelcome and sharp. I remembered the smell of pine needles mixed with cheap bug spray, the forced enthusiasm of the counselors. A wave of profound unease washed over me, making my hands tremble slightly as I picked up the photo again. It felt like a stain on my otherwise orderly life.

[story 2]

I found myself pacing the breakroom, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead mirroring the frantic rhythm in my chest. When Mark mentioned the camp, a coldness seeped into my bones. He described the activities with an almost manic energy, detailing forced bonding exercises and overly saccharine group songs. I leaned against the counter, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, trying to physically contain the rising tide of revulsion. "That place," I said, my tone flat, devoid of warmth, "it sounds… aggressively wholesome. I wouldn't have volunteered for that kind of environment." The sheer saccharine nature of the description made my skin crawl. I took a long, shallow breath, the air tasting stale, and immediately turned away, focusing intently on the chipped paint of the coffee machine.

[story 3]

The email chain was short, just a few exchanged messages about a shared childhood experience. When I read the details of the summer camp—the mandatory group activities, the forced camaraderie—a sour feeling tightened my jaw. I felt an immediate urge to scrub my hands raw. I reread the descriptions, focusing on the forced smiles and the manufactured closeness. My gaze kept flicking away from the screen, my eyes narrowed slightly as if trying to filter out something unpleasant. "Seriously?" I finally managed, the word coming out sharper than intended. My posture stiffened, every muscle in my body seeming to tense up against the unwelcome memory of that enforced togetherness.
